meta.title: W163 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W163 engine — M111, M112, M113, OM612 and OM628. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and body‑on‑frame rust advice. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes M-Class W163 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W163 engine typically costs £1,800–£9,000 supply only [MB-QUOTE], depending on engine — the volume ML 320 sits at £2,200–£3,800. Fitting adds 10–18 hours labour at specialist rates due to the body‑on‑frame SUV packaging. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W163 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112.942 (ML 320) — a 3.2‑litre V6 with simple 3‑valve technology, no balance‑shaft issues, and a proven 200,000‑mile reputation. The M112 is widely considered one of the most reliable Mercedes engines ever built. For diesel buyers, the OM612 (ML 270 CDI) is a proven five‑cylinder unit — but budget for EGR and injector maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes M-Class W163?

acceptedAnswer.text: M112 thermostat and valve cover gaskets (V6 petrols), OM612 EGR valve clogging and injector seal leaks (diesel), M113 valve stem seals and oil leaks (V8 petrols), and — chassis‑specific — body‑on‑frame rust and subframe corrosion on all cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes M-Class W163 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the W163 is a robust SUV with a proven powertrain line‑up. The M112 V6 and M113 V8 are among the most reliable engines Mercedes ever built; the OM612 diesel is a proven five‑cylinder unit. The biggest reliability challenge is age‑related: rust on the body‑on‑frame chassis, not engine failure. Choose carefully and always inspect the chassis rails.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W163 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an OM612 (ML 270 CDI) without documented EGR and injector service history is the one we'd approach cautiously, given the £500–£1,200 repair cost for EGR and injector seal issues. A neglected M113 V8 without oil leak history is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W163 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overheating or coolant loss (M112 thermostat failure), black smoke or loss of power (OM612 EGR clogging), blue smoke on start‑up (M113 valve stem seals), oil leaks or burning smell (valve cover gaskets), or engine management light. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes M-Class W163 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and rust condition. Against a well‑kept £5,000 W163, a £2,200–£3,800 M112 recon is 44–76% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon is 73–127% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. Always inspect the chassis rails before committing to any major repair — rust is the real killer on the W163.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W163 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 and M113 V8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — many examples have covered 250,000+ miles. The OM612 diesel is similarly durable — 250,000+ miles is achievable with EGR and injector maintenance. The W163's body‑on‑frame structure means the body often outlasts the powertrain.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W163 and the W164 M-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W163 is the first‑generation M‑Class (1997–2005, this page), built on a body‑on‑frame chassis. The W164 is the second generation (2005–2011), built on a unibody platform. The W164 is lighter, more refined and more car‑like to drive. The W163 is more rugged and better suited to off‑road use — but the body‑on‑frame construction brings rust risks.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W163 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation; also stamped on the engine block (visible from below — the M112/M113 is at the front, OM612 on the side). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes M-Class W163 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Mercedes M-Class W163 — from the 2.3-litre M111 four-cylinder to the 5.4-litre M113 AMG V8, plus the OM612 five-cylinder and OM628 V8 diesels that powered the first Mercedes SUV. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the 2001 facelift that brought V8 power to the range. Backed by 276+ real UK W163 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes M-Class W163 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W163 specifically — from the entry‑level 2.3‑litre four‑cylinder to the 5.4‑litre AMG V8, plus the OM612 five‑cylinder and OM628 V8 diesels.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All eight engine families are in Case B — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any Mercedes M111, M112, M113, OM612 or OM628 family. Ratings are derived from the Known Failure Patterns table in EM_Proprietary_Data (where available) and cross‑referenced against specialist/owner sources. The M112 V6 and M113 V8 are widely regarded as robust and durable; the OM612 five‑cylinder diesel is a proven unit but has EGR and injector seal issues; the OM628 V8 diesel is rare but well‑regarded. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The W163 is the first generation of the Mercedes‑Benz M‑Class, launched in February 1997 as the brand's first-ever SUV. Built on a body‑on‑frame chassis and produced in Tuscaloosa, Alabama, it was available exclusively as a five‑door SUV with seating for five or seven passengers. A significant facelift in 2001 brought new powertrains — the 5.0‑litre V8 ML 500 and the 4.0‑litre V8 diesel ML 400 CDI — along with updated styling. At the top of the range sat the ML 55 AMG — a 5.4‑litre V8‑powered SUV that could hit 62 mph in 6.4 seconds.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W163 (body‑on‑frame) • Predecessor: None (first M‑Class) • Successor: W164 • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (SUV only)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W163 M‑Class generated an estimated 276+ UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED — PLACEHOLDER], concentrated on the ML 270 CDI diesel and ML 320 petrol V6 as these ageing SUVs enter affordable second‑hand ownership.

items.quote: The 4.0‑litre V8 diesel — 250 bhp, 560 Nm of torque, and the most powerful diesel ever fitted to a W163. Smooth, effortless, and the only V8 diesel in the range. It's rare — only produced from 2001 — but those who have one love it.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 diesel torque and rarity — but be prepared for specialist maintenance.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre V6 — 215–218 bhp, 310 Nm of torque, and the most common engine in the UK W163 parc. The M112 is widely considered one of the most reliable engines Mercedes ever built — simple 3‑valve technology, no balance‑shaft issues, just smooth V6 power. If you can find one with a clean history, the powertrain itself is unlikely to give you grief.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most reliable, best‑supported petrol W163 with proven 200,000‑mile longevity.

items.quote: The 2.7‑litre five‑cylinder diesel — 163 bhp, 370 Nm of torque. While generally reliable, the OM612 has known issues: EGR valve failure, black smoke and loss of power, injector seal leaks ('black death'), and camshaft wear. The 5‑cylinder is a proven unit but needs careful maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand the OM612's quirks and budget for EGR and injector maintenance.

items.quote: The 5.4‑litre V8 is a masterpiece, but when it fails — valve stem seals, timing chain, or the 5‑speed auto that sits behind it — the repair bills are in a different league. Low production numbers mean parts are specialist‑only. The ML 55 AMG is rare and collectable, but maintenance costs reflect that.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who understand that 347 bhp comes with a six‑figure maintenance budget.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre V6 is the sweet spot of the W163 range — proven reliability, the most common engine in the UK parc, reasonable running costs, and the cheapest recon cost of any six‑cylinder. It's the default choice for good reason.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement and proven reliability without the V8 running costs.

items.quote: The 3.7‑litre V6 — 235–245 bhp, 345 Nm of torque — is often overshadowed by the ML 320 and the V8s, but it's the same durable M112 family with more power. It replaced the ML 320 in 2002 and offers the best power‑to‑weight ratio of the non‑AMG petrols.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want maximum M112 V6 power without the V8's running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Overlooked Gem — more power, same reliability
V8 Power — 292 bhp, V8 running costs
Watch — EGR and injector issues common
Rare Diesel — V8 torque, specialist costs

economicsRule.title: The W163 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200–£3,800 M112 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 W163 sits at 28–127% of the car's value. On a sub‑£3,500 W163, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £5,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 44–76% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, and always budget for body‑on‑frame rust inspection — the biggest non‑engine cost on this generation.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W163 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: ML 320 (M112.942) — durable V6, common parts, proven 200,000‑mile longevity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W163 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: ML 55 AMG (M113.981) — V8 running costs, AMG parts prices, specialist‑only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W163 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: ML 320 (M112.942) — reliable V6, the most common engine in the UK parc, and the cheapest six‑cylinder to maintain.

problems.title: M112 V6 Thermostat & Valve Cover Gaskets
problems.recommendation: On any M112 W163, inspect the thermostat and valve cover gaskets regularly — £300–£600 now is far cheaper than the £2,200–£3,800 replacement that follows if oil leakage causes damage.

problems.title: OM612 EGR & Injector Seal Leaks (ML 270 CDI)
problems.recommendation: On any OM612 W163, inspect the EGR valve and injector seals regularly — £500–£1,200 now is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if injector seal failure causes cylinder head damage.

problems.title: W163 Body‑on‑Frame Rust & Subframe Corrosion
problems.recommendation: Inspect the chassis rails and subframe mounting points carefully before purchase — £500–£1,000 now is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000 repair that follows if corrosion spreads. This is the biggest non‑engine issue on the W163.

problems.title: M113 V8 Valve Stem Seals & Oil Leaks (ML 430 / ML 500 / ML 55 AMG)
problems.recommendation: On any M113 W163, monitor oil consumption and inspect for leaks regularly — £1,000–£2,000 now is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,000 replacement that follows if oil loss causes bearing damage.

replacementCosts.h2: W163 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline — no Engine Page exists yet for any M111, M112, M113, OM612 or OM628 family. These costs are derived from the EM_Proprietary_Data Table A — Engine Replacement Economics for comparable engines, adjusted for the W163's body‑on‑frame SUV labour access and marked [MB-QUOTE] accordingly. All figures are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W163 five‑door SUV only. The W163 was the first‑generation M‑Class and was available exclusively as a five‑door SUV. The successor W164 launched in 2005.
